meta.title: US Independence
meta.description: The American Revolution and the birth of a new nation. Explore how different choices during this pivotal period could have led to dramatically different outcomes for America and the world.
meta.historical_context: By 1776, tensions between Great Britain and its American colonies had reached a breaking point. Years of taxation without representation, military occupation, and restrictive policies had created a revolutionary movement that would change the course of world history.
meta.divergence_point: Declaration of Independence and Revolutionary War

george-washington.name: George Washington
george-washington.description: Leader of the Continental Army and first President of the United States

benjamin-franklin.name: Benjamin Franklin
benjamin-franklin.description: Diplomat who secured French alliance and helped draft the Declaration of Independence

king-george-iii.name: King George III
king-george-iii.description: British monarch during the American Revolution

thomas-jefferson.name: Thomas Jefferson
thomas-jefferson.role: Primary author of Declaration of Independence
thomas-jefferson.description: Third President and primary author of the Declaration of Independence

british-victory.title: British Victory Timeline
british-victory.description: Revolutionary War fails, America remains a British colony until the 1850s
british-victory.divergence_description: French alliance never forms, Washington is captured at Valley Forge
british-victory.present_day_status: The United States exists as the American Commonwealth, a semi-autonomous region within a reformed British Empire. Parliamentary democracy with the British monarch as ceremonial head of state. Economy similar to our timeline but with stronger ties to Commonwealth nations.

washington-captured-1778.title: Washington Captured at Valley Forge
washington-captured-1778.description: British surprise attack captures George Washington and decimates Continental Army

colonial-submission-1779.title: Colonial Submission
colonial-submission-1779.description: Remaining colonial leaders surrender to British forces
colonial-submission-1779.impact: End of organized resistance, return to colonial status

harsh-punishments-1780.title: Harsh British Punishments
harsh-punishments-1780.description: Britain imposes severe penalties on rebellious colonies
harsh-punishments-1780.impact: Heavy taxation, military occupation, restricted freedoms

delayed-independence.short_term: America remains under British rule with harsh restrictions
delayed-independence.long_term: Independence delayed by 75 years, achieved through negotiation rather than revolution
delayed-independence.global_impact: French Revolution might not have occurred without American example

no-french-revolution.trigger: No American example of successful revolution
no-french-revolution.consequence: French Revolution delayed or takes different form

french-america.title: French America Timeline
french-america.description: France directly intervenes and America becomes a French protectorate
french-america.divergence_description: France commits massive military forces early in the war, demanding political control in return
french-america.present_day_status: The French American Republic is a bilingual nation with strong cultural ties to France. Constitutional democracy with a civil law system. Major power in both Atlantic and Pacific, with special relationship to EU rather than Britain.

massive-french-intervention-1777.title: Massive French Military Intervention
massive-french-intervention-1777.description: France sends 50,000 troops and large naval fleet to America
massive-french-intervention-1777.impact: Overwhelming military superiority against British forces

treaty-protectorate-1778.title: Treaty of French Protectorate
treaty-protectorate-1778.description: America agrees to become French protectorate in exchange for independence from Britain
treaty-protectorate-1778.impact: Political control shifts from Britain to France

french-cultural-influence.short_term: French language and culture become dominant in America
french-cultural-influence.long_term: Bilingual French-English speaking nation with strong European ties
french-cultural-influence.global_impact: Catholic rather than Protestant cultural influence in North America

stronger-france.trigger: France gains enormous territory and resources
stronger-france.consequence: France remains dominant European power through 19th century

confederated-states.title: Loose Confederation Timeline
confederated-states.description: Weak confederation of independent states, no strong federal government
confederated-states.divergence_description: Articles of Confederation never replaced, states remain largely independent
confederated-states.present_day_status: The Confederated States of America operates as a loose federal system with very strong state governments. Similar to Switzerland or early EU model. Economic development similar to our timeline but with stronger ties to Commonwealth nations.

constitutional-convention-fails-1787.title: Constitutional Convention Fails
constitutional-convention-fails-1787.description: States cannot agree on new constitution, Articles of Confederation remain
constitutional-convention-fails-1787.impact: Weak central government continues, states maintain independence

delayed-development.short_term: Slow economic development due to trade barriers between states
delayed-development.global_impact: America remains primarily agricultural power longer

different-civil-war.trigger: Weaker federal government cannot prevent secession
different-civil-war.consequence: Multiple smaller conflicts instead of single civil war

colonial-uprising.title: The Boston Tea Party (1773)
colonial-uprising.text: You are a merchant in Boston. The Sons of Liberty are gathering at the harbor. They plan to dump British tea into the ocean. The Redcoats are watching. What do you do?

join.text: Put on a disguise and join them. Liberty or death!
join.consequence: You become a marked man, but a hero to the patriots. The revolution begins.

report.text: Report them to the Governor. This is destruction of property!
report.consequence: The British arrest the ringleaders. The rebellion is stifled for now.

watch.text: Watch from the shadows. Keep your head down.
watch.consequence: The event happens without you. You survive, but have no voice in what comes next.

reconciliation-effort.title: The King's Peace (1774)
reconciliation-effort.text: Thanks to loyal subjects like you, the "Tea Party" was thwarted. But anger remains. You are invited to London to speak to Parliament.

demand-rights.consequence: Parliament laughs at you. You return home radicalized.

accept-status.text: Accept a minor trade deal and pledge loyalty.
accept-status.consequence: You prevent war, but America remains a colony forever.

continental-congress.title: Independence Hall (1776)
continental-congress.text: Philadelphia. The delegates are debating. Jefferson has drafted a Declaration of Independence. Some say it is treason. How do you vote?

vote-yes.text: Vote YES. "We must all hang together!"
vote-yes.consequence: The Declaration is signed. War is declared.

vote-no.text: Vote NO. We cannot win against the British Empire.
vote-no.consequence: The motion fails. The colonies remain divided. The British pick them off one by one.

long-island.title: Disaster at Long Island (1776)
long-island.text: The British have landed a massive army in New York. Washington's forces are outnumbered and outflanked. The river is at your back.

retreat-night.text: Retreat across the river under cover of fog.
retreat-night.consequence: A miraculous escape! The army lives to fight another day.

stand-ground.text: Stand and fight! "No retreat, no surrender!"
stand-ground.consequence: Your brave stand is doomed. The army is surrounded and crushed.

washington-captured.title: The End of the Revolution (1776)
washington-captured.text: General Washington is in chains. The Continental Army is no more. The King offers a pardon to all who swear allegiance.

submit.text: Swear allegiance to King George III.
submit.consequence: The revolution is over. Order is restored.

guerilla.text: Flee to the hills and fight a guerilla war.
guerilla.consequence: You fight for years, but without leadership, it is just banditry.

valley-forge.title: Winter of Despair (1777)
valley-forge.text: Valley Forge. Cold, hunger, and smallpox are killing your men. Baron von Steuben offers to train them, but morale is breaking.

inoculate.text: Order mass inoculation against smallpox. Risky but necessary.
inoculate.consequence: Many die, but the epidemic stops. The army emerges stronger.

attack-philly.text: Attack Philadelphia to get warm quarters.
attack-philly.consequence: The attack is a disaster. The army is destroyed in the snow.

french-alliance.title: The French Court (1778)
french-alliance.text: Ben Franklin is in Paris. The King of France offers an alliance, but demands the US become a protectorate under French law.

accept-protectorate.text: Accept. We need their navy at any cost.
accept-protectorate.consequence: The French fleet arrives and crushes the British. But now you answer to Versailles.

demand-equal.consequence: Franklin charms the court. They agree to an equal alliance!

yorktown-finale.title: Victory at Yorktown (1781)
yorktown-finale.text: Cornwallis is trapped. The British surrender. You have won the war! Now you must win the peace.

federal-constitution.text: Draft a strong Constitution with a central government.
federal-constitution.consequence: The United States of America is born. A new superpower rises.

keep-confederation.text: Keep power with the states. No more kings!
keep-confederation.consequence: The states bicker and fight. The union is weak.
